title: Stage 3 — The Topping Phase
subtitle: The most dangerous stage to hold through

lessons.title: Chart Characteristics
lessons.visual_caption: Infosys (INFY) — Weekly chart showing the full S2→S3→S4 cycle. Notice how the 30-week SMA flattens at the top (Stage 3) before rolling over into Stage 4.
lessons.content:
Stage 3 is where most investors give back their Stage 2 gains by holding too long. Learning to recognise it early can protect everything you built in Stage 2.

Trend Direction: The uptrend ends and price moves sideways again — but now at a high level after a long advance.

Price Action: Price stops making higher highs and begins to swing up and down in wide, erratic moves. It looks volatile and confusing.

Price Boundaries: Price oscillates between a high resistance ceiling and a critical support floor. These swings are wider and more violent than Stage 1.

The Master Line: The 30-week SMA loses its upward slope and flattens. Volatile bars whip back and forth straight through it.

Volume: Heavy and chaotic, with big spikes on down-weeks — the distribution pattern from Module 2.

Look at the Infosys chart. After the long Stage 2 advance, see how the 30-week SMA begins to flatten near the top. Wide, violent swings appear. This is Stage 3. It preceded a severe Stage 4 decline.

→ Sideways price with wide violent swings
→ 30-week SMA flattens at a high level
→ Heavy volume on down-weeks (distribution)
→ Confusing, erratic price action

lessons.title: Market Psychology in Stage 3
lessons.visual_caption: TCS — Weekly chart. The long Stage 2 advance followed by a clear Stage 3 top. Volume spiked on down-weeks as institutions distributed shares while retail was still buying.
lessons.content:
Stage 3 is the most psychologically deceptive stage. Everything looks fine from the news — while the smart money is quietly leaving.

Balance of Power: Buyers and sellers are evenly matched again — but this time at a high price.

The Big Investors: Institutions quietly offload large blocks to retail buyers. This is distribution. They sell slowly to avoid crashing the price immediately.

The Public: Retail is excited and the news is full of positive stories about the company — strong earnings, new contracts, analyst upgrades. This good news is exactly what allows institutions to sell at the top.

The cruel trap: The news is best when the stock is in Stage 3. Retail buys the positive headlines. Institutions sell into that retail buying. By the time the news turns bad, Stage 4 has already begun.

The TCS chart above shows this clearly. After a huge Stage 2 advance, TCS entered Stage 3 topping. The 30-week SMA flattened. Volume spiked on down-weeks as distribution happened.

→ Best news = worst time to buy
→ Institutions sell while retail is still excited
→ Distribution is invisible to most investors
→ The warning signs are in the chart, not the news

lessons.title: Action Rules — What To Do in Stage 3
lessons.content:
Stage 3 requires action, not patience. The time for patience was Stage 1. Now you must protect what you earned.

First Warning Signal — Act Immediately: When the 30-week line flattens and the price swings turn wide and violent, sell half (50%) of your position immediately to lock in profits.

Why sell only half? Selling half locks in significant gains while keeping a small position in case the stock somehow resumes a Stage 2 advance. You are protecting profits, not predicting the future.

Protection Rule: Do not buy pullbacks in Stage 3. A Stage 3 pullback is not a buying opportunity — it is a stock that has already lost its uptrend.

The mistake everyone makes: Holding through Stage 3 because the news is still good. Waiting for one more high. Telling yourself it is just a temporary pause in the Stage 2 advance. This is how small gains become losses.

Weinstein's clear instruction:
→ First warning (30-week SMA flattens) = sell half
→ Do not add to positions in Stage 3
→ Prepare for the possibility of a full Stage 4 breakdown

Note: The short-term trader's full-exit trigger for this phase is covered in Module 8.

→ 30-week SMA flattening = sell 50% immediately
→ Protect profits, do not chase more
→ Never buy Stage 3 pullbacks
→ The time to act is early, not after the damage

questions.question: What does price action look like during Stage 3?
options: It rises smoothly in a tight upward line
It falls continuously, making lower lows every week
It moves sideways with wide, violent swings
It stands still as volume drops to zero
questions.explanation: Stage 3 is high-volatility chop — price stops trending up and swings erratically inside a wide horizontal range. The swings are much wider and more violent than Stage 1.

questions.question: What happens to the 30-week SMA in Stage 3?
options: It loses its upward slope and flattens
It turns into a different indicator
questions.explanation: With price no longer advancing, the 30-week average stalls and flattens out horizontally. A flattening 30-week SMA after a long advance is Weinstein's first warning signal.

questions.question: What are institutions doing during Stage 3 distribution?
options: Buying as many shares as possible
Waiting for a further 50% drop before selling
Quietly selling shares to excited retail buyers
Holding all shares and waiting for Stage 2 to resume
questions.explanation: Institutions use the retail excitement and positive news coverage to unload large positions. They sell slowly to avoid crashing the price — which is why Stage 3 can last months.

questions.question: What is the core action rule when a stock enters Stage 3?
options: Buy more because the news is positive
Sell half the position immediately to protect profits
Short the stock with all available capital
Sit idle and ignore the chart for six months
questions.explanation: Selling half locks in significant gains while keeping a small position in case of a renewed advance. This protects profits from the distribution that is likely underway.
